Let’s See….. Let’s see if I have this straight. Donald Trump set a deadline of 8pm April 7 for Iran to stop attacking ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz. Over the Easter weekend, Trump posted an obscene threat to Iran promising Iranians will be “living in hell” if they do not comply by the deadline. On the morning of April 7, Trump posted another threat , promising, “A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again.” A little more than an hour before the deadline, with some reports saying that long-range bombers were already enroute to the Middle East from their bases in the United States, Trump announced an agreement for a two-week ceasefire. Trump and his supporters see this as an example of superior deal-making. Keep your opponent under pressure and uncertain of your next step. Critics see it as just another example of what they call Trump’s TACO tendency, as in, “Trump Always Chickens Out.” The whiplash alone will kill us. Trump justified his attack on Iran with the claim that the country was very close to having a nuclear warhead and the missiles to deliver it. Nothing has been released to back up that claim. Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman have reported in The New York Times details about Trump’s decision-making process. They say their information comes from reporting for their forthcoming book, “Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump.” The article indicates there was considerable skepticism from most of Trump’s national security staff about the decision. Strong support came only from Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth . Hegseth has argued that the sole purpose of the military is “lethality,” specifically, the efficient killing of enemies to break their will. Hegseth has also characterized the conflict with Iran as a religious battle. There is no mention of these arguments in Trump’s post announcing the cease fire. There is a reference to the “COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING of the Strait of Hormuz….” From that it is clear Trump’s mind is now focused on the price of gasoline , soaring to well over four dollars a gallon on average and as high as five dollars a gallon in some parts of the United States. It is the price at the pump that will have the greatest impact on voters in November . Trump clearly fears he and his Republican enablers will suffer. History says he will be right. So now, and only now, the closing of the shipping lane which transports twenty percent of the world’s energy supply is of concern. Ben Rhodes , a deputy national security advisor to President Barak Obama, observes that Trump’s newly announced deal is a move to “reopen a Strait that was open before the pointless war he started”. It is not clear that Trump’s two-week cease fire has even accomplished that goal. Iran’s response, which Trump himself posted, says “safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz will be possible via coordination with Iran’s Armed Forces and with due consideration of technical limitations. Those “technical limitations” appear to include forcing ships to travel within Iran’s territorial waters and reportedly will include the payment of a toll to Iran . Trump has stated that perhaps the answer is for the United States and Iran to share that toll for passage. In any event, Iran has kept the Strait closed, claiming it predicated opening the Strait to shipping is predicated on Israel ending its military action in Lebanon. Trump has denied any such promise was even discussed, let alone part of the agreement. More negotiations are planned. The Strait of Hormuz is not like the Suez Canal, or the Erie Canal, or the St. Lawrence Seaway. Those are manmade structures located completely within a sovereign nation. The Strait of Hormuz is a natural waterway , connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. It is bordered not only by Iran, but also by Oman and the United Arab Emirates. As such it is an international waterway. There is an established route in its center. No tolls are charged and interfering with the right of passage violates international law. Not that Iran cares about any of that. As for Trump, he has admitted that he “goes with his gut.” He doesn’t take advice because he believes he is always the smartest person in the room. He also knows or cares little about history or he would know Iran has blocked or threatened to block the Strait many times before. Only now, with his MAGA acolytes screaming to their congressmen, is he paying attention. Who benefits from Trump’s actions? Believe it or not, Iran and Russia . In an attempt to push gas prices down, Trump is temporarily lifting sanctions on Russian oil that is currently at sea, allowing it to be shipped to buyers around the world. The sanctions were designed to pressure Russia into ending its military campaign in Ukraine . Oil and gas exports are Russia’s primary source of revenue. Russian will rake in the profits, which it will use to further the war it is fighting with Ukraine. As incredible as that seems, for his next act Trump waived sanctions on Iranian oil purchases at sea for 30 days. This will pour hundreds of millions of dollars into Iran’s treasury. Which they can use to buy and build more missiles, drones, and bombs to fire on America soldiers and bases in the region. And, naturally, at Israel. All to ease surging oil prices driven in the US. You can’t make this stuff up. ##### #economics #election #Gasoline #Iran #Israel #MiddleEast #Oil #politics #Russia #Sanctions #StraitOfHormuz #Trump #Ukraine #war

Hello, I’m Nada from Gaza City, and some of you here may already know me. I graduated in Information Technology and worked in this field since the day I graduated, but I lost my job after the war. I was also a writer in Arabic, escaping into words whenever reality became too heavy, finding pieces of myself within writing. I also love drawing cartoon characters in charcoal, and I will be sharing some of my work soon—like a small window through which I look out at the world. For quite some time now, I have been carrying the responsibility of my family alone, without support or a provider. My husband is critically injured and requires ongoing medical treatment, and my children do not even have their basic needs met. We are now living in a tent after losing our home and everything that once held us together. I try to appear steady, to hold myself together no matter how broken I feel inside, and to play the role of strength for a family that has nothing left to lean on except my patience. But behind this strength, there is a woman who is silently exhausted, enduring more than she can bear. Today, I am not asking for much… only your support, a sincere word, or a helping hand extended in this heavy time—something that can ease this burden that has become greater than my capacity to carry. I hope I am not alone on this road. L'Unità: Arresto di Mohammed Hannoun, per la Cassazione le soffiate degli 007 israeliani non si possono usare La Cassazione conferma che non si possono usare nelle inchieste giudiziarie in Italia atti trasmessi dai servizi segreti israeliani e ordina al Tribunale del Riesame di Genova di rivalutare la posizione di quattro indagati tra i quali l’architetto Mohammed Hannoun arrestati con l’accusa di aver finanziato Hamas e tuttora in carcere in attesa della prossima udienza tra una decina di giorni. La Cassazione ha inoltre dichiarato inammissibile il ricorso della procura di Genova tendente a far ritornare in carcere gli indagati che il Riesame aveva liberato. Almeno per adesso è una vittoria importante su tutta la linea per gli avvocati della difesa. I legali Nicola Canestrini, Sandro Clementi, Fausto Gianelli, Elisa Marino, Gilberto Pagani , Pierfrancesco Poli, Marina Prosperi, Nabil Ryah, Federico Riboldi, Dario Rossi, Flavio Rossi Albertini Giuseppe Sambataro, Fabio Sommovigo, Emanuele Tambuscio, Gianluca Vitale e Samuele Zucchi sottolineano che in attesa delle motivazioni della Suprema Corte “pare evidente che il Tribunale dovrà riconsiderare le posizioni degli indagati per i quali era stato provato solo l’invio a Gaza di aiuti alimentari e di denaro destinato ad attività umanitarie e di sostegno alla popolazione civile”. Per gli avvocati difensori “è importante anche che la Cassazione  abbia dichiarato inammissibile il ricorso della procura tendente a far rientrare nel processo le carte dei servizi segreti di Israele”. Gli atti israeliani sono stati ritenuti “anonimi e carenti delle garanzie processuali richieste dal nostro ordinamento”. Si tratta per i legali di “un passaggio necessario nell’inchiesta ‘Domino’ che aveva portato all’arresto di nove persone in diverse regioni italiane”. E ovviamente dipinte come “terroristi” dai mezzi di informazione. Sugli esiti della vicenda resta grande incertezza perché non esiste giurisprudenza su associazione sovversiva finanziatrice di Hamas. Le organizzazioni islamiste infatti sono una questione molto diversa. Bene o male Hamas governa Gaza. Resta il fatto che la Cassazione ha deciso che non c’erano indizi sufficientemente gravi per procedere agli arresti. Detention of Mohammed Hannoun, according to the Court of Cassation, the whispers of the 007 Israeli agents cannot be used. The Court of Cassation confirmed that documents transmitted by Israeli intelligence services cannot be used in judicial investigations in Italy and orders the Genoa Court of Appeal to reassess the status of four suspects, including architect Mohammed Hannoun, who are currently in prison awaiting their next hearing in about ten days, accused of financing Hamas. The Court of Cassation also ruled the Genoa prosecutor’s appeal inadmissible, seeking to reinstate the suspects’ imprisonment after the Court of Appeal had released them. At least for now, it’s an important victory for the defense lawyers. Lawyers Nicola Canestrini, Sandro Clementi, Fausto Gianelli, Elisa Marino, Gilberto Pagani, Pierfrancesco Poli, Marina Prosperi, Nabil Ryah, Federico Riboldi, Dario Rossi, Flavio Rossi Albertini Giuseppe Sambataro, Fabio Sommovigo, Emanuele Tambuscio, Gianluca Vitale and Samuele Zucchi emphasize that “pending the Supreme Court’s motivations, it appears evident that the Court will need to reconsider the positions of the suspects for whom only the delivery to Gaza of food aid and money intended for humanitarian activities and support for the civilian population was proven.” For the defense lawyers, “it is also important that the Court of Cassation has ruled the prosecutor’s appeal inadmissible, seeking to include in the trial the documents from Israeli intelligence services.” The Israeli documents were deemed “anonymous and lacking the procedural guarantees required by our legal system.” These are “a necessary step in the ‘Domino’ investigation, which led to the arrest of nine people in various regions of Italy.” And, of course, painted as “terrorists” by the media. The arrest of Mohammad Hannoun has been annulled: he was accused of financing Hamas. The decision by the Appeals Court. The Court of Cassation has overturned the arrest of Mohammad Hannoun, the Palestinian activist and architect accused of financing Hamas through his Association for Solidarity with the Palestinian People (ASPSPP). The 64-year-old man had been arrested at the end of December along with six other people. Two of them, Raed El Salahat and Khalil Abu Deiah, had already been released while the arrests of Hannoun, Ra’ed Dawoud, Yaser Elasaly and Ryad Albunstanji were confirmed. For all four, the Court has deferred the decision to a section of the Appeals Court in Genoa. Pending the decision, Hannoun will remain in prison in Terni. The defense team said they were satisfied with the Court of Cassation’s decision. “Pending the motivations of the Supreme Court, it seems evident that the court will have to reconsider the positions of the investigated, for which only the sending of food aid and money intended for humanitarian activities in support of the civilian population was proven.” According to the lawyers, “it is also important that the Court of Cassation has declared inadmissible the appeal by the Public Prosecutor’s Office of Genoa, aimed at involving the documents transmitted by Israeli intelligence services, which had already been excluded by the Appeals Court.” Hannoun has lived in Genoa since 1983. The Prosecutor began to suspect possible links with Hamas at the end of the 1990s but the accusations were dropped. The Association’s activities had always been under surveillance and between 2021 and 2023, several banks and payment operators had frozen the accounts after some reports from the Israeli government. The foundation had already been accused in the past of organizing fundraising campaigns disguised as charitable activities for humanitarian purposes, but Hannoun has always denied it.
